An unusual mid winter tornado outbreak occurred over southwest and central Missouri.  31 tornadoes struck the region within a 15 hour time frame on 7 January into early morning 8 January.  Two tornadoes intensified to EF-3 status while five tornadoes caused EF-2 damage.  All other tornadoes during this outbreak were surveyed and given EF-0 and EF-1 status.  Multiple training supercells spawned most of these tornadoes that occurred along the Interstate 44 corridor.  Toward the end of this episode, a broken squall line spawned numerous EF-0 and EF-1 tornadoes across the southern Missouri Ozarks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey revealed that an EF-0 tornado briefly touched down at the Thousand Hills Golf Resort.  Three condomeniums sustained minor damage.  The tornado also caused damage at a mobile home park in Hollister.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Marginally severe thunderstorms, mainly producing hail up to the size of quarters, also caused flash flooding in several areas.  A slow moving frontal system was positioned over southern Missouri, allowing for unusual amounts of moisture and modest instability to develop along and south of this feature.  Mostly non-severe thunderstorms developed at random over southern Missouri.  However, two tornadic supercells approached south central Missouri from Arkansas, but failed to produce tornadoes within the Springfield, Missouri county warning area.

Training supercell thunderstorms produced large hail up to the size of baseballs.  Wind damage along with flooding also occurred from these storms, which mainly impacted extreme southeast Kansas and southwest Missouri.  A few other isolated storms impacted central and south central Missouri.

A few strong to marginally severe thunderstorms impacted extreme southeast Kansas and far western Missouri.  One storm that tracked across Barton County produced nickel size hail along with a brief tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm wind gusts downed a couple of trees near the intersection of Highway 71 and Highway 126.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe storm with heavy rain occurred in Poplar Bluff. The storm developed along a surface trough that extended from eastern Missouri into Arkansas. At 500 mb, a weak shortwave embedded in northwest flow contributed to the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to half-dollar size hail occurred throughout most of Poplar Bluff. The hail damaged plants on the north side of the city and broke at least one window.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few strong to marginally severe thunderstorms impacted southern Missouri the morning of Halloween.  Mainly small hail and frequent lightning was observed with these storms.  One storm impacted 14 students at Buffalo High School.  A lightning strike knocked down a large tree limb which fell on the students causing minor injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fourteen Buffalo High School students were injured from a large tree limb falling to the ground.  This tree limb fell due to lightning striking the tree.  All of the injuries from the students were minor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced reports of large hail across the area,|during the evening hours of 6/27/2008.
A broken squall line caused widespread damaging winds over portions of the Missouri Ozarks.  Damage was mainly to trees, powerlines, and a few structures.  Localized flash flooding was also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A telephone poll was knocked over from severe thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved south through the region, triggering showers and thunderstorms.  Some of the storms produced severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several power lines northwest of Granville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two clusters of showers and thunderstorms impacted mainly extreme southeast Kansas and far southwest Missouri. Flash flooding occurred with both clusters. Two to six inches of rain fell on saturated soil conditions, leading to a quick onset of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tributary of Knobby Creek flooded over a section of Highway DD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm tracked north to south along the Highway 65 corridor producing damaging winds and hail up to the size of baseballs.  As this storm moved into the Springfield area, an EF-1 tornado developed and caused damage to furniture stores on the east side of town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A section of Highway K at its intersection with Turnback Creek was flooded and impassable to motorists.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon and evening severe thunderstorms triggered by a northward moving warm front produced hail, wind damage, a couple of small tornadoes, and localized flash flooding across Northeast and East Central Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm spotters estimated 60 mph wind gusts at Meramec and Compton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few early evening thunderstorms dropped hail up to 1 inch on parts of the St. Louis Metropolitan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 3/4 inch hail in the Sunset Hills area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms impacted southern Missouri with large hail, damaging winds, and a couple of tornadoes.  The two tornadoes were confirmed by a National Weather Service storm survey over Douglas County, including northern sections of the city of Ava.
A strong cold front moved south through the region, triggering showers and thunderstorms.  Some of the storms produced severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down onto Route K west of New Hartford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the area, and caused a severe weather outbreak across the area. Large hail and damaging wind gusts were observed. An isolated tornado was also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree limbs reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms containing large hail impacted numerous locations across the Missouri Ozarks Region.
A cold front moved across the area, and caused a severe weather outbreak across the area. Large hail and damaging wind gusts were observed. An isolated tornado was also observed.
A cold front moved across the area, and caused a severe weather outbreak across the area. Large hail and damaging wind gusts were observed. An isolated tornado was also observed.

A warm front moved slowly north across southeast Missouri during the afternoon. Large clusters of thunderstorms produced very heavy rain and a few reports of large hail. Rainfall amounts averaged from 2 to 4 inches during the 24-hour period, with isolated higher amounts up to 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route FF was closed from U.S. Highway 67 to Route 34 due to the high level of Lake Wappapello. Route C was closed from Route E to Route 34.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the area, and caused a severe weather outbreak across the area. Large hail and damaging wind gusts were observed. An isolated tornado was also observed.
Severe thunderstorms containing large hail impacted numerous locations across the Missouri Ozarks Region.

A large squall line of thunderstorms impacted all locations of extreme southeast Kansas and the Missouri Ozarks.  Several locations experienced severe weather, mainly in the form of damaging straight line winds.  However, two brief tornadoes occurred in McDonald and Barry counties.  Meanwhile, there were a few observations of large hail and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were damaged and a few roofs lost shingles from severe thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms developed along a cold front as it moved through the area on April 22nd.
A warm front lifted northward through the region...triggering showers and thunderstorms.  Some of the storms became severe and/or caused flash flooding.
Early morning heavy rains caused a few flash warnings to be issued on 09/13/2008.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vehicle was swept off of Highway 139, due to fast flowing water. The driver was able to swim to safety.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains were observed over portions of Missouri, around the 1st and 2nd of March, as a strong upper level storm system moved across the region. This resulted in minor to moderate flooding, of area creeks and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grand River at Gallatin crested at 30.21 feet, or 4.21 feet above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Steady amounts of light to moderate rain caused streams and creeks to slowly rise out of their banks across much of extreme southeast Kansas and the Missouri Ozarks.  Widespread rainfall amounts from 3 March and 4 March ranged from one to four inches.  The greatest rainfall totals occurred over Taney, Ozark, Douglas, and Howell counties where significant flooding of secondary roads caused several school districts to cancel school on 4 March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A section of Highway AB approximately six miles northeast of Mansfield was flooded and impassable to motorists for a couple of days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains were observed over portions of Missouri, around the 1st and 2nd of March, as a strong upper level storm system moved across the region. This resulted in minor to moderate flooding, of area creeks and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grand River near Sumner crested at 35.12 feet, or 9.12 feet above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours of 22 July.  Damaging winds and a few observations of large hail resulted from these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were snapped or uprooted by severe thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few marginally severe thunderstorms impacted portions of central Missouri during the evening hours of 25 July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts knocked over several large trees and power poles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms erupted overnight 3 June into the morning of 4 June.  Areas north of Interstate 44 were mainly impacted by these storms.  Large hail, damaging straight line winds, and one tornado occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several low water crossings across the county became impassable to motorists.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the region with severe storms developing ahead of the front.

Eleven tornadoes and significant flash flooding occurred in response to several clusters of thunderstorms impacting the Missouri Ozarks.  The ground was saturated from record rainfall in both the months of February and March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey revealed that an EF-2 tornado impacted a section of Dallas County, including the city of Buffalo.  Approximately 40 homes were heavily damaged or destroyed, and three people were injured.  The path of destruction extended over an industrial plant and flipped airplanes at the Buffalo Airport. Maximum wind speeds were estimated at 120 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms containing large hail impacted numerous locations across the Missouri Ozarks Region.
A strong cold front moved through the region with severe storms developing ahead of the front.
A severe thunderstorm moved across portions of northwest and central Missouri, during the early morning hours of April 25, 2008. There were several reports of hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor damage to structures was reported, and several trees were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front that lifted north across the region, generated a few severe thunderstorms with large hail, along and north of Highway 36.

Scattered severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and early evening of 8 July.  These storms mainly produced damaging downburst winds, knocking over trees and power lines.  Approximately 30 boat docks near Grand Glaze Bridge were damaged overturned on Lake of the Ozarks from a more intense microburst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downburst winds knocked trees down across Highway U approximately one mile west of Highway 65.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of strong to severe thunderstorms developed over southeast Nebraska and impacted central and southwest Missouri during the late morning and afternoon hours of 15 June.  The storms produced sporadic wind damage along with hail up to two inches in diameter in the city of Ozark.  Hail to the size of golfballs was observed at the National Weather Service in Springfield, Missouri.

A cluster of severe thunderstorms developed along a west to east oriented cold front during the evening of 5 August.  Several observations of large hail and damaging winds occurred from these storms.  Nearly five inches of rain fell over a rural area along the Phelps and Maries county line.  This excessive rain caused significant flash flooding in this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tributary of Brush Creek flooded a section of Highway V southwest of Flemington.  A motorist attempted to cross the flooded roadway and was washed off.  No injuries occurred from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms developed along a west to east oriented cold front during the evening of 5 August.  Several observations of large hail and damaging winds occurred from these storms.  Nearly five inches of rain fell over a rural area along the Phelps and Maries county line.  This excessive rain caused significant flash flooding in this area.

This episode of severe weather and flooding occurred during the overnight period of 22 June, and into the morning hours of 23 June.  A nocturnal low level jet developed over the southern and central Plains and interacted with a stalled front extending from east central Missouri to southeast Kansas.  Hail to the size of baseballs occurred from Cherokee County, Kansas to Jasper County, Missouri.  Several observations of wind gusts to 70 mph also occurred over southeast Kansas.

Severe thunderstorms with very heavy rains and hail, pummelled portions|of north central and central Missouri, late in the evening of June 24,|2008, into the morning hours of June 25, 2008. Torrential rains reached|around 9 inches in and around Linneus. The heavy rains aggravated|the ongoing flooding of area creeks and rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway U was under water one mile north of Highway P.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few strong to severe thunderstorms developed causing a swath of large hail and damaging winds along the Highway 13 corridor.  A tornado also tracked from Schell City to north of El Dorado Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person was injured after being struck by baseball size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few strong to severe thunderstorms developed causing a swath of large hail and damaging winds along the Highway 13 corridor.  A tornado also tracked from Schell City to north of El Dorado Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were downed from severe thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of strong to severe thunderstorms developed over southeast Nebraska and impacted central and southwest Missouri during the late morning and afternoon hours of 15 June.  The storms produced sporadic wind damage along with hail up to two inches in diameter in the city of Ozark.  Hail to the size of golfballs was observed at the National Weather Service in Springfield, Missouri.
Severe thunderstorms brought hail to portions of the area, during the|evening hours of June 3, 2008.
A series of strong storm systems in March raised the Mississippi River above flood stage. The record-setting heavy rainfall on the 18th and 19th was the primary cause of the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding of the Mississippi River occurred. At the Cape Girardeau gage, the river crested at 41.04 feet on March 24. Flood stage there is 32 feet, and moderate flooding begins at 37 feet. The river backed into several creeks. Large areas of agricultural land were flooded. Spring planting was not yet underway, so crop losses were negligible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced reports of large hail across the area,|during the evening hours of 6/27/2008.

A cluster of severe thunderstorms developed along a west to east oriented cold front during the evening of 5 August.  Several observations of large hail and damaging winds occurred from these storms.  Nearly five inches of rain fell over a rural area along the Phelps and Maries county line.  This excessive rain caused significant flash flooding in this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts downed numerous trees and tore the roof off of one home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the landfall of Hurricane Gustav along the Louisiana coast, Gustav's extra-tropical circulation tracked directly into southern Missouri.  The remnant moisture from Gustav created widespread rainfall amounts ranging from two to six inches across the region.  Pre-existing dry soil conditions and thick summertime vegetation limited flooding from becoming widespread and significant.  However, some localized flooding was observed.  One tornado occurred in the community of Chain of Lakes in southeast Barry County. Minor damage occurred and the tornado was rated as an EF-0.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of rain gradually fell over Barry County.  A few locations flooded including a section of Farm Road 1230 southwest of Shell Knob, a section of Highway Y at its intersection with Flat Creek, a section of Highway C at its intersection with Flat Creek, and a section of Highway U at its intersection with Flatt Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms increased to severe strength across northeast Kansas, by the late morning hours of June 2, 2008. The complex continued to move southeast into west central Missouri. Very large hail, damaging thunderstorm winds, and flash flooding, accompanied the storms. Hail sizes were in the half dollar to golfball size range, with baseball size hail reported near Rich Hill. In addition to the large hail, thunderstorm wind gusts estimated between 60 and 80 miles an hour, felled trees and even tore a roof off a home near Trading Post, Kansas.
A complex storm system, brought severe thunderstorms with a few|tornadoes, high winds, hail, and flooding rains to the area, late on|June 5, 2008, into the early morning hours of June 6, 2008.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief EF0 tornado touchdown in open country at 1823CST. No damage or injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several boundaries from previous activity remained over the region with very unstable conditions.  This helped to trigger another round of severe thunderstorms and heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to three inches of rain fell on already saturated soils in Knox county.  Several secondary roads were flooded including Route K just southwest of Colony, where it goes over the Middle Fabius River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large mesoscale complex developed over the region producing severe weather and very heavy rain on already saturated soils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 2 and 3 inches of rain fell in a short amount of time on already saturated soils causing flash flooding.  Several roads in the Hallsville area were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large mesoscale complex developed over the region producing severe weather and very heavy rain on already saturated soils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to ping pong size fell in Stephens and areas just to the south of town.  Several vehicles sustained minor hail damage and a couple of windows on 3 machine sheds were knocked out by the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms formed during the heat of the afternoon, aided by a weak surface trough that extended from northern Arkansas northeast across the Lower Ohio Valley. Tropical moisture combined with the slow movement of the storms resulted in locally very heavy rain. Some of the moisture was from the remnants of Hurricane Dolly.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple houses were struck by lightning. About one third of the residents in the city were without power due to lightning strikes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storm total rainfall amounts ranged from one to six inches during the evening and overnight hours of 13 September into the morning of 14 September.  Widespread flooding of small streams, creeks, and main stem rivers resulted, which was only one of two primary hazards that occurred this night.  The other hazard included a belt of non-thunderstorm high winds that likely gusted up to 65 mph at times.  These winds mainly damaged trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of rain fell over Phelps County resulting in flooding of small streams, creeks, and one main stem river.  A few specific locations that flooded included a section of Highway E northwest of Rolla, a section of Highway Y, a section of Highway P, and several streets in the community of Newburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved northeastward through the area triggered numerous showers and thunderstorms causing widespread flash flooding and a few isolated severe events.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 3 and 5 inches of rain fell in a short amount of time on already saturated soils causing flash flooding.  Numerous roads in the Centralia area were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an extremely hot and muggy day, in which temperatures soared into the 90s and dew points rose into the 70s, across Missouri, a weak stationary front and sinking upper level disturbance from the north, provided enough energy to allow several severe thunderstorms to form, during the evening and overnight hours of Monday, July 21st.  Thunderstorms rapidly formed in areas northeast of Chillicothe and Brookfield, with thunderstorms reaching severe levels in less than 10 minutes time. These severe thunderstorms, quickly produced damaging winds, and even a few tornado touchdowns.

An unusual mid winter tornado outbreak occurred over southwest and central Missouri.  31 tornadoes struck the region within a 15 hour time frame on 7 January into early morning 8 January.  Two tornadoes intensified to EF-3 status while five tornadoes caused EF-2 damage.  All other tornadoes during this outbreak were surveyed and given EF-0 and EF-1 status.  Multiple training supercells spawned most of these tornadoes that occurred along the Interstate 44 corridor.  Toward the end of this episode, a broken squall line spawned numerous EF-0 and EF-1 tornadoes across the southern Missouri Ozarks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported down along Highway 106 for a six to eight mile stretch near Highway 106 between Summersville and Alley Spring.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms impacted southern Missouri with large hail, damaging winds, and a couple of tornadoes.  The two tornadoes were confirmed by a National Weather Service storm survey over Douglas County, including northern sections of the city of Ava.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts downed a large tree across Highway 76.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of strong to severe thunderstorms developed over southeast Nebraska and impacted central and southwest Missouri during the late morning and afternoon hours of 15 June.  The storms produced sporadic wind damage along with hail up to two inches in diameter in the city of Ozark.  Hail to the size of golfballs was observed at the National Weather Service in Springfield, Missouri.

A supercell thunderstorm tracked north to south along the Highway 65 corridor producing damaging winds and hail up to the size of baseballs.  As this storm moved into the Springfield area, an EF-1 tornado developed and caused damage to furniture stores on the east side of town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gust downed a large tree near the Millwood Golf Course.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very hot and muggy day in which temperatures soared into the upper 80s to middle 90s, and dew points rose into the 70s across much of Missouri and Kansas, a weak cold front interacted with an upper level disturbance, moving across the northern plains and pushed south into central and southern Missouri. The combination of the frontal boundary and the upper level energy, provided enough lift to allow several strong to severe thunderstorms to form, during the late afternoon and evening hours of Thursday, August 28th. Large hail and damaging winds were reported with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limb was reported down and blocking traffic on Maple Road. Tree limbs were also reported down on Merrill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front slid across the region...triggering numerous thunderstorms...so of which became severe.

Excessive rainfall developed over southern Missouri during the evening of 17 March.  A line of training convection assumed a position roughly along a line from Anderson to Ozark to Licking.  This convection expanded with time, eventually covering nearly all of extreme southeast Kansas and the Missouri Ozarks.  Moderate to heavy rain continued into the overnight period and did not stop until the morning of 19 March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of rain fell over Lawrence County.  All locations that typically experience flooding during periods of excessive rainfall were flooded.  One fatality occurred in Kelly Creek near Monett.  A 19 year old male died after walking into the swift current.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Eleven tornadoes and significant flash flooding occurred in response to several clusters of thunderstorms impacting the Missouri Ozarks.  The ground was saturated from record rainfall in both the months of February and March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Saturated antecedent conditions existed prior to this period of excessive rainfall.  Some regional locations experienced record rainfall totals from February and March.  One to three inches of rain fell across the county causing widespread flash flooding of low water crossings, county roads, and low lying areas near creeks and rivers.  Ultimately, all locations that typically flood during periods of excessive rainfall were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm tracked north to south along the Highway 65 corridor producing damaging winds and hail up to the size of baseballs.  As this storm moved into the Springfield area, an EF-1 tornado developed and caused damage to furniture stores on the east side of town.

A few clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms impacted southern Missouri with large hail, damaging winds, and a couple of tornadoes.  The two tornadoes were confirmed by a National Weather Service storm survey over Douglas County, including northern sections of the city of Ava.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey revealed that an EF-0 tornado touched down along Highway AJ approximately one half of a mile east of Highway 76.  The tornado lifted near the intersection of Highway 76 and County Road 508.  Damage from the tornado was limited to numerous trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three to six inches of rain fell over portions of southwest Missouri during the morning of 28 June.  This excessive rain in conjunction with pre-existing saturated soil conditions caused significant flooding.  Severe thunderstorms also occurred, as numerous observations of wind damage and large hail were relayed to the National Weather Service.  One tornado caused damage to a home and surrounding trees in rural McDonald County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to three inches of rain fell over Webster County.  Flooding of numerous low lying roads and low water crossings resulted.  Locations that flooded included city streets in Rogersville, city streets in Marshfield, roads in the Northview area, a section of Highway OO, a section of Bell Ford Road north of the James River, a section of Panther Creek Road north of Fordland, a section of Highway J west of Pomme De Terre Creek, a section of Interstate 44 at mile marker 91, a section of Highway B one mile south of Northview, the intersection of Highway FF and Panther Creek, a section of Highway Z south of Highway 60, a section of Ridge Road, a section of Jarrett Branch Road east of Cedar Ridge Road, and a section of Mill Dam Road near its intersection of Heritage Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm tracked north to south along the Highway 65 corridor producing damaging winds and hail up to the size of baseballs.  As this storm moved into the Springfield area, an EF-1 tornado developed and caused damage to furniture stores on the east side of town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey revealed that an EF-1 tornado impacted a section of east Springfield.  The tornado and its damaging rear flank downdraft tracked along the Highway 65 corridor, damaging numerous businesses and a few homes.  Furniture Row sustained damage as a large section of the roof fell into the Sofa Mart.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small cluster of thunderstorms produced marginally severe hail and wind gusts over Greene, Polk, Taney, and Camden counties.  Urban flooding across the city of Springfield also resulted as a few city streets became impassable to motorists.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Urban flooding occurred over several locations within the city of Springfield.  Specific locations included the intersection of Grant and Commercial streets, the intersection of Kearney and Barnes, and the a section of Farm Road 164.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This episode of severe weather and flooding occurred during the overnight period of 22 June, and into the morning hours of 23 June.  A nocturnal low level jet developed over the southern and central Plains and interacted with a stalled front extending from east central Missouri to southeast Kansas.  Hail to the size of baseballs occurred from Cherokee County, Kansas to Jasper County, Missouri.  Several observations of wind gusts to 70 mph also occurred over southeast Kansas.
